name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
ٱلَّذِينَ ٱسْتَجَابُوا۟ لِلَّهِ وَٱلرَّسُولِ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَآ أَصَابَهُمُ ٱلْقَرْحُ ۚ لِلَّذِينَ أَحْسَنُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ وَٱتَّقَوْا۟ أَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ (١٧٢)
```

```english
Those who responded to Allah and the Apostle [even] after they had been wounded—for those of them who have been virtuous and Godwary there shall be a great reward. (172)
```

```arabic
ٱلَّذِينَ قَالَ لَهُمُ ٱلنَّاسُ إِنَّ ٱلنَّاسَ قَدْ جَمَعُوا۟ لَكُمْ فَٱخْشَوْهُمْ فَزَادَهُمْ إِيمَـٰنًا وَقَالُوا۟ حَسْبُنَا ٱللَّهُ وَنِعْمَ ٱلْوَكِيلُ (١٧٣)
```

```english
—Those to whom the people said, ‘All the people have gathered against you, so fear them.’ That only increased them in faith, and they said, ‘Allah is sufficient for us, and He is an excellent trustee.’ (173)
```

```arabic
فَٱنقَلَبُوا۟ بِنِعْمَةٍ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَفَضْلٍ لَّمْ يَمْسَسْهُمْ سُوٓءٌ وَٱتَّبَعُوا۟ رِضْوَٰنَ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ ذُو فَضْلٍ عَظِيمٍ (١٧٤)
```

```english
So they returned with Allah’s blessing and grace, untouched by any harm. They pursued the pleasure of Allah, and Allah is dispenser of a great grace. (174)
```

```arabic
إِنَّمَا ذَٰلِكُمُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ يُخَوِّفُ أَوْلِيَآءَهُۥ فَلَا تَخَافُوهُمْ وَخَافُونِ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (١٧٥)
```

```english
That is only Satan frightening [you] of his followers! So fear them not, and fear Me, should you be faithful. (175)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses are connected to the verses regarding the Battle of Uhud (غزوة أحد), as suggested by His saying: **مِنْ بَعْدِ ما أَصابَهُمُ الْقَرْحُ** — "after they had been smitten by the wound" (3:172), while He had said regarding that battle: **إِنْ يَمْسَسْكُمْ قَرْحٌ فَقَدْ مَسَّ الْقَوْمَ قَرْحٌ مِثْلُهُ** — "If a wound afflicts you, a like wound has already afflicted those people" (3:140).

His saying, the Exalted: **الَّذِينَ اسْتَجابُوا لِلَّهِ وَالرَّسُولِ** — "Those who responded to Allah and the Apostle" (3:172). The terms *istijabah* (الاستجابة) and *ijabah* (الإجابة) carry the same meaning—as has been said—which is to be asked for something and to respond with acceptance.

Perhaps the mention of both Allah and the Messenger, despite the sufficiency of mentioning only one in this context, is because during the Battle of Uhud they had disobeyed both Allah and the Messenger. As for Him, the Exalted, they disobeyed Him by fleeing and turning their backs, which Allah had forbidden them from doing while commanding them to engage in *jihad* (الجهاد) — *the striving in the way of Allah*. As for the Messenger, they disobeyed him by violating the order he issued to the archers to remain at their posts, and when they were climbing the mountain while he was calling them from their rear, yet they did not respond to his call. Thus, when they responded in this subsequent encounter, their response to Allah and the Messenger was placed in contrast to that previous encounter.

His saying: **لِلَّذِينَ أَحْسَنُوا مِنْهُمْ وَاتَّقَوْا أَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ** — "For such of them as were virtuous and God-conscious, there shall be a great reward" (3:172). He restricted the promise to only some of those who responded, because responding is an outward act (فعل ظاهري) that does not necessarily entail the reality of virtue (الإحسان) and godwariness (التقوى) — *the innate state of being conscious of Allah's presence* — upon which the great reward is predicated. This is part of the wondrous precision of the Quranic discourse, where one matter does not distract it from another. From this, it becomes clear that this group was not entirely sincere toward Allah in their affair; rather, there were those among them who were not virtuous or God-conscious (متقياً) enough to deserve the great reward from Allah, the Glorified. It is sometimes said that the word "of" (مِنْ) in His saying "of them" (مِنْهُمْ) is explanatory (بيانية), as has been said regarding His saying, the Exalted: **مُحَمَّدٌ رَسُولُ اللهِ وَالَّذِينَ مَعَهُ أَشِدَّاءُ عَلَى الْكُفَّارِ** — "Muhammad, the Apostle of Allah, and those who are with him are hard against the faithless..." until He says: **وَعَدَ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ مِنْهُمْ مَغْفِرَةً وَأَجْراً عَظِيماً** — "Allah has promised those of them who have faith and do righteous deeds forgiveness and a great reward" (48:29). However, this is an interpretation (تأول) that is refuted by the context (السياق).

It also becomes clear that the praise Allah, the Glorified, bestows upon them in His saying: **الَّذِينَ قالَ لَهُمُ النَّاسُ** — "Those to whom the people said..." (3:173) until the end of the verses, is of the nature of attributing the description of a part to the whole through a linguistic consideration (عناية لفظية).

His saying, the Exalted: **الَّذِينَ قالَ لَهُمُ النَّاسُ إِنَّ النَّاسَ قَدْ جَمَعُوا لَكُمْ** — "Those to whom the people said, 'All the people have mustered against you...'" (3:173). The term "people" (الناس) refers to human individuals without considering the specific characteristics that distinguish one from another. The first "people" mentioned are different from the second. The second refers to the enemy who was gathering forces. As for the first, they were the discouragers and hinderers who said what they said to dissuade the believers from going out to fight the polytheists (المشركين). Thus, the second "people" refers to the polytheists, while the first "people" were those whose hands were against the believers and whose eyes were upon them. The apparent meaning of the verse is that they were a group, not a single individual. This supports the view that the verses were revealed concerning the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) expedition with the remaining companions after Uhud in pursuit of the polytheists, rather than the story of the "Lesser Badr" (بدر الصغرى). Both accounts will be discussed in the forthcoming "Traditionary Discourse."

His saying: **قَدْ جَمَعُوا لَكُمْ** — "have mustered against you," meaning they have gathered their forces to fight you a second time (and Allah knows best).

His saying: **فَزادَهُمْ إِيماناً** — "it only increased them in faith" (3:173). This is because it is in the nature of a human being that when he is forbidden from something he desires and is determined upon, if he does not have a good opinion of the one forbidding him, it acts as an incitement. It triggers his faculties and strengthens his resolve. The more he is insistently barred, the more he insists on proceeding with what he intends. This effect is even stronger if the one being forbidden sees himself as being in the right and justified in his actions. Therefore, whenever a blamer censured the believers regarding the command of Allah or a hinderer tried to stop them, they only increased in the strength of their faith (الإيمان) and the intensity of their resolve and courage.

It is also possible that the increase in their faith was because such reports confirmed the revelation they had received—that they would be persecuted for the sake of Allah until their affair was completed by Allah's permission, and He had promised them victory, and victory only comes through encounter and combat.

His saying: **وَقالُوا حَسْبُنَا اللهُ وَنِعْمَ الْوَكِيلُ** — "and they said, 'Allah is sufficient for us, and He is an excellent trustee'" (3:173). Meaning, Allah is enough for us. The root of *hasb* (الحسب) comes from *hisab* (الحساب) — *calculation* — because sufficiency is according to the calculation of need. This is a reliance on Allah based on faith (الإيمان), setting aside the external causes (الأسباب الخارجية) that operate within the Divine Custom (السنة الإلهية). The "Trustee" (الوكيل) is the one who manages affairs on behalf of a person. Thus, the content of the verse returns to the meaning of His saying: **وَمَنْ يَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى اللهِ فَهُوَ حَسْبُهُ إِنَّ اللهَ بالِغُ أَمْرِهِ** — "And whoever puts his trust in Allah, He will suffice him. Indeed Allah carries through His command" (65:3). For this reason, He followed the saying **وَقالُوا حَسْبُنَا اللهُ وَنِعْمَ الْوَكِيلُ** with His saying: **فَانْقَلَبُوا بِنِعْمَةٍ مِنَ اللهِ وَفَضْلٍ لَمْ يَمْسَسْهمْ سُوءٌ** — "So they returned with Allah’s blessing and grace, and no evil touched them" (3:174), so that it might be a confirmation of His promise. Then He praised them for following His pleasure, saying: "and they followed Allah’s pleasure; and Allah is dispenser of a boundless grace."

## (A Discourse on Trust in Allah)

The reality of the matter is that the execution of will and the attainment of the goal in the material realm (نشأة المادة) require both natural causes (أسباب طبيعية) and spiritual causes (أسباب روحية). When a person intends to enter into an important matter and prepares the necessary natural causes, nothing stands between him and his objective except the failure of spiritual causes—such as weakness of will, fear, grief, recklessness, greed, folly, or ill-suspicion. These are significant and pervasive factors. However, when he puts his trust (التوكل) in Allah, the Glorified, he connects himself to a cause that is absolutely invincible—the Cause that is above every cause. Consequently, his will becomes so strong that it cannot be overcome by any opposing or conflicting spiritual causes, leading to attainment and felicity.

There is another dimension to trust in Allah (التوكل) that relates to its effect through supernatural means (خوارق العادة), as is apparent from His saying: **وَمَنْ يَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى اللهِ فَهُوَ حَسْبُهُ إِنَّ اللهَ بالِغُ أَمْرِهِ** — "And whoever puts his trust in Allah, He will suffice him. Indeed Allah carries through His command" (65:3). A portion of the discussion related to this was previously covered in the discourse on miracles (الإعجاز).

His saying, the Exalted: **ذلِكُمُ الشَّيْطانُ يُخَوِّفُ أَوْلِياءَهُ** — "That is only Satan frightening his friends" (3:175). The apparent meaning of the verse is that the reference is to the "people" who said what they said to them. Thus, this is one of the instances where the Quran applies the term "Satan" (الشيطان) to a human being, as is evident from His saying: **مِنْ شَرِّ الْوَسْواسِ الْخَنَّاسِ الَّذِي يُوَسْوِسُ فِي صُدُورِ النَّاسِ مِنَ الْجِنَّةِ وَالنَّاسِ** — "from the evil of the sneaking whisperer, who whispers into the breasts of mankind, from among the jinn and the humans" (114:4-6). This is supported by His saying immediately after: **فَلا تَخافُوهُمْ** — "so do not fear them" (3:175), meaning the people who spoke to you, because "that is Satan." We shall discuss this meaning in a way that unveils its true reality, if Allah wills.

## (Narrative Discussion)

The narrations regarding the Battle of Uhud (غزوة أحد) are extremely numerous, yet they differ significantly in various aspects of the story, which may lead to skepticism regarding them. The greatest discrepancies are found in the narrations concerning the occasions of revelation (أسباب النزول) for many of the verses of this story—which are nearly sixty verses—for their matter is indeed strange. A contemplative observer cannot help but conclude that different schools of thought (المذاهب) have breathed their own spirits into these narrations to speak through them for their own benefit. This is our excuse for omitting them in this discussion; whoever seeks them should consult the compendiums of *hadith* (جوامع الحديث) and the extensive commentaries (مطولات التفاسير).

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Abu al-Duha who said: "The verse **وَيَتَّخِذَ مِنْكُمْ شُهَداءَ** — 'and that He may take martyrs from among you' (3:140) was revealed, and seventy of them were killed on that day: four from the Emigrants (المهاجرين)—including Hamzah ibn Abd al-Muttalib, Mus'ab ibn Umayr of the Banu Abd al-Dar, Shammas ibn Uthman al-Makhzumi, and Abd Allah ibn Jahsh al-Asadi—and the rest were from the Helpers (الأنصار)."

I say: The apparent meaning of the narration is that Abu al-Duha took the term "martyrs" (الشهداء) in the verse to mean those killed in battle. Most commentators have followed this view. However, it was mentioned in the previous commentary that there is no evidence for this in the apparent text of the Book; rather, the apparent meaning is that "martyrs" refers to the witnesses of deeds (شهداء الأعمال).

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **أَمْ حَسِبْتُمْ أَنْ تَدْخُلُوا الْجَنَّةَ وَلَمَّا يَعْلَمِ اللهُ** — "Do you suppose that you will enter paradise though Allah has not yet known..." (3:142), it is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) that he said: "Indeed, Allah knew what He would bring into existence before He brought it into existence—while they were in the realm of particles (ذر)—and He knew who would strive from those who would not, just as He knew He would cause His creation to die before He caused them to die, yet He did not see their death while they were alive."

I say: This is an allusion to what was previously mentioned—that there is a distinction between knowledge before creation and actual knowledge (العلم الفعلي), which is the act itself, and that the intended meaning is not the knowledge before creation.

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَقَدْ كُنْتُمْ تَمَنَّوْنَ الْمَوْتَ** — "You certainly used to wish for death..." (3:143): "When Allah informed the believers of what He had prepared for their martyrs at Badr in their stations in Paradise, they yearned for that and said: 'O Allah, show us a battle in which we may be martyred.' So Allah showed them the day of Uhud, but they did not stand firm except for those whom Allah willed. That is His saying: **وَلَقَدْ كُنْتُمْ تَمَنَّوْنَ الْمَوْتَ**." I say: This meaning is also narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Ibn Abbas, Mujahid, Qatadah, al-Hasan, and al-Suddi.

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, he (peace be upon him) said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) went out on the day of Uhud, and someone saw him in that state and began saying to whomever he met: 'The Messenger of Allah has been killed, save yourselves!' When they returned to Medina, Allah revealed: **وَما مُحَمَّدٌ إِلَّا رَسُولٌ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِنْ قَبْلِهِ الرُّسُلُ** — 'Muhammad is but an apostle; [other] apostles have passed before him...' (3:144) until His saying: **انْقَلَبْتُمْ عَلى أَعْقابِكُمْ** — 'will you turn back on your heels?' (3:144), meaning to disbelief (الكفر); **وَمَنْ يَنْقَلِبْ عَلى عَقِبَيْهِ فَلَنْ يَضُرَّ اللهَ شَيْئاً** — 'Whoever turns back on his heels will not harm Allah in the least' (3:144)."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Jarir and Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from al-Rabi' regarding the verse: "That was on the day of Uhud when they were afflicted by killing and wounds, and they called out for the Prophet of Allah, saying: 'He has been killed.' Some people among them said: 'If he were a prophet, he would not have been killed.' But some of the elite companions of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Fight for what your Prophet fought for until Allah grants you victory or you join him.' It was mentioned to us that a man from the Emigrants passed by a man from the Helpers who was wallowing in his blood and said: 'O so-and-so, do you know that Muhammad has been killed?' The Helper replied: 'If Muhammad has been killed, he has delivered the message; so fight for your religion.' Then Allah revealed: **وَما مُحَمَّدٌ إِلَّا رَسُولٌ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِنْ قَبْلِهِ الرُّسُلُ أَفَإِنْ ماتَ أَوْ قُتِلَ انْقَلَبْتُمْ عَلى أَعْقابِكُمْ** — 'Muhammad is but an apostle; [other] apostles have passed before him. If he dies or is slain, will you turn back on your heels?' (3:144), meaning: you would apostatize as disbelievers after your faith."

In the same source, Ibn Jarir narrated from al-Suddi: "It spread among the people on the day of Uhud that the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) had been killed. Some of the 'people of the rock' said: 'If only we had a messenger to go to Abd Allah ibn Ubayy so he could get us a guarantee of safety from Abu Sufyan. O people, Muhammad has been killed, so return to your people before they come and kill you.' Anas ibn al-Nadr said: 'O people, if Muhammad has been killed, the Lord of Muhammad has not been killed. So fight for what Muhammad fought for. O Allah, I apologize to You for what these people say, and I disassociate myself from what they have brought.' Then he drew his sword and fought until he was killed. Then Allah revealed: **وَما مُحَمَّدٌ إِلَّا رَسُولٌ**."

I say: These meanings have been narrated through many other chains.

In *al-Kafi*, from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him): "Ali was afflicted with sixty wounds on the day of Uhud. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) ordered Umm Sulaym and Umm Atiyyah to treat him. They said: 'We no sooner treat one place than another bursts open, and we fear for him.' The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and the Muslims entered to visit him while he was one continuous wound. He began wiping him with his hand and saying: 'A man who has met this for the sake of Allah has indeed been tested and has fulfilled his duty.' Every wound the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) wiped would heal. Ali said: 'Praise be to Allah that I did not flee nor turn my back.' Allah thanked him for that in two places in the Quran: **وَسَيَجْزِي اللهُ الشَّاكِرِينَ** — 'and Allah will soon reward the grateful' (3:144), and **سَنَجْزِي الشَّاكِرِينَ** — 'We will soon reward the grateful' (3:145)."

I say: This means Allah thanked him for his steadfastness, not for his saying "Praise be to Allah."

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) that he read: **وكأين من نبي قتل معه ربيون كثير** — "How many a prophet there has been with whom many a godly man fought" (3:146). He said: "Thousands upon thousands." Then he said: "Yes, by Allah, they are killed."

I say: This reading and meaning are narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Ibn Mas'ud and others. It is narrated from Ibn Abbas that he was asked about the word *ribbiyyun* (ربيون) and he said: "Multitudes."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Abd ibn Humayd and Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Mujahid regarding **مِنْ بَعْدِ ما أَراكُمْ ما تُحِبُّونَ** — "after He had shown you what you love" (3:152): "Allah gave the believers victory over the polytheists until the women of the polytheists were riding every mount, easy or difficult [to flee]. Then the turn of fortune went against them because of their disobedience to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)."

In the same source, Ibn Ishaq, Ibn Rahwayh, Abd ibn Humayd, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and al-Bayhaqi in *al-Dala'il* narrated from al-Zubayr: "I saw myself with the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) when fear intensified upon us. Allah sent down sleep upon us, such that there was not a man among us but his chin was on his chest. By Allah, I could hear the words of Mu'attib ibn Qushayr—I heard them as if in a dream—'If we had any say in the matter, we would not have been killed here.' I memorized it from him. Regarding that, Allah revealed: **ثُمَّ أَنْزَلَ عَلَيْكُمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ الْغَمِّ أَمَنَةً نُعاساً** — 'Then He sent down to you, after the grief, a security—a slumber...' (3:154) until His saying: **ما قُتِلْنا هاهُنا** — 'we would not have been killed here' (3:154), referring to the words of Mu'attib ibn Qushayr."

I say: This meaning has been narrated from al-Zubayr ibn al-Awwam through many chains.

In the same source, Ibn Mandah in *Ma'rifat al-Sahabah* narrated from Ibn Abbas regarding His saying: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ تَوَلَّوْا مِنْكُمْ يَوْمَ الْتَقَى الْجَمْعانِ** — "Those of you who turned their backs on the day when the two hosts met..." (3:155): "It was revealed concerning Uthman, Rafi' ibn al-Mu'alla, and Harithah ibn Zayd."

I say: Something similar is narrated through several chains from Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf, Ikrimah, and Ibn Ishaq, and in some of them, Abu Hudhayfah ibn Utbah, al-Walid ibn Utbah, Sa'd ibn Uthman, and Utbah ibn Uthman are added.

In any case, the mention of Uthman and those counted with him by name is by way of mentioning an instance (المصداق); otherwise, the verse was revealed concerning all the companions who turned back and disobeyed the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). What specifically pertains to Uthman is that he and those with him fled until they reached al-Jal'ab (a mountain in the direction of Medina near al-Aghwas), where they stayed for three days before returning to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). He said to them: "You went far and wide in it."

As for his companions in general, narrations are numerous that they turned back almost to the last man, and none remained with the Messenger of Allah except two men from the Emigrants and seven from the Helpers. Then the polytheists attacked the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and the Helpers were killed one by one defending him until none of them remained with him.

It is narrated that those who stood firm with him were eleven, or eighteen, and even thirty has been narrated, which is the weakest of the narrations.

Perhaps this discrepancy is due to the different information available to the narrators and other factors. What is indicated by the narrations of Nusaybah al-Maziniyyah's defense of him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is that there was no one with him at that moment; those who had stood firm and not fled were occupied with fighting. The narrators do not agree on anyone except Ali (peace be upon him), and perhaps Abu Dujanah al-Ansari Simak ibn Kharashah as well, though he first fought with the sword of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and then shielded the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) with his own body when his companions had scattered, warding off arrows with his shield and his back until he was severely wounded (may Allah be pleased with him).

As for the rest of his companions, some joined him once they recognized him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and knew he had not been killed, and others joined him after a while. These are the ones upon whom Allah sent down slumber, though Allah, the Exalted, pardoned them all. You have already learned the meaning of "pardon" (العفو) in the previous commentary. Some commentators mentioned that the meaning of pardon in this verse is that He, the Exalted, turned the polytheists away from them so they did not annihilate them or kill them to the last man.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Adi and al-Bayhaqi in *al-Shu'ab* narrated with a good chain from Ibn Abbas: "When **وَشاوِرْهُمْ فِي الْأَمْرِ** — 'and consult them in the matter' (3:159) was revealed, the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'As for Allah and His Messenger, they are certainly independent of it, but Allah made it a mercy for my community. Whoever among them consults will not lack right guidance, and whoever abandons it will not lack error.'"

In the same source, al-Tabarani in *al-Awsat* narrated from Anas: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'He who seeks the best from Allah (استخار) will not fail, and he who consults (استشار) will not regret.'"

In *Nahj al-Balaghah*: "He who is autocratic in his opinion perishes, and he who consults men shares in their intellects."

Also in it: "Consultation is the very essence of guidance, and he who is autocratic in his opinion takes a risk."

In *al-Safi*, from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "No loneliness is more desolate than vanity, and no support is more reliable than consultation."

I say: The narrations regarding consultation (المشاورة) are very numerous. Their scope is what is permissible for the consultant to do or leave according to prevailing considerations. As for the established Divine laws (الأحكام الإلهية), there is no room for consultation in them, nor is there permission for anyone to change them; otherwise, the variation of occurring events would abrogate the speech of Allah, the Exalted.

In *al-Majalis*, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "The pleasure of people cannot be possessed, and their tongues cannot be controlled. Did they not accuse him on the day of Badr of taking for himself a red velvet cloak from the spoils? Until Allah revealed the cloak and cleared His Prophet of treachery, and revealed in His Book: **وَما كانَ لِنَبِيٍّ أَنْ يَغُلَ** — 'It is not for a prophet to be untrustworthy' (3:161)."

I say: al-Qummi mentioned this in his commentary, adding: "A man came to the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him) and said: 'So-and-so has hidden a red velvet cloak, so dig it up there.' The Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him) ordered that place to be dug, and the cloak was brought out."

This meaning and similar ones have been narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* through many chains. Perhaps the meaning of the verse being revealed regarding it is that the verse alludes to it; otherwise, the context of the verses indicates they were revealed after the Battle of Uhud, as previously explained.

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him): "Whoever misappropriates (غل) something will see it on the Day of Resurrection in the Fire; then he will be tasked to enter it and bring it out of the Fire."

I say: This is a subtle derivation from His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَنْ يَغْلُلْ يَأْتِ بِما غَلَّ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ** — "and whoever is untrustworthy will bring his trust on the Day of Resurrection" (3:161).

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **هُمْ دَرَجاتٌ عِنْدَ اللهِ** — "They have [various] degrees with Allah" (3:163), from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "Those who followed the pleasure of Allah are the Imams (الأئمة); they are, by Allah, degrees with Allah for the believers. Through their guardianship (ولاية) and their love for us, Allah multiplies their deeds for them and raises for them the high degrees. And those who earned the wrath of Allah are those who denied the right of Ali and the right of the Imams from us, the People of the House (أهل البيت); thus they earned the wrath of Allah."

I say: This is of the nature of "conceptual flow and application" (الجري والانطباق).

In the same source, from Imam al-Rida (peace be upon him): "A degree is [the distance] between the heaven and the earth."

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi* as well, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **أَوَلَمَّا أَصابَتْكُمْ مُصِيبَةٌ قَدْ أَصَبْتُمْ مِثْلَيْها** — "What! When a misfortune smote you, while you had already smitten [the enemy] with twice as much..." (3:165), from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "The Muslims had smitten one hundred and forty men at Badr: they killed seventy and took seventy captive. When the day of Uhud came, seventy men from the Muslims were smitten. They were grieved by that, so the verse was revealed."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Abi Shaybah, al-Tirmidhi—who graded it as *hasan*—Ibn Jarir, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ali: "Gabriel came to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and said: 'O Muhammad, Allah has disliked what your people did in taking captives, and He has commanded you to give them a choice between two things: either they come forward and their necks are struck, or they take ransom on the condition that an equal number of them will be killed.' The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) called the people and mentioned that to them. They said: 'O Messenger of Allah, they are our kinsmen and our people; we will take their ransom so we may be strengthened for fighting our enemy, and an equal number of us will be martyred later—there is nothing in that we dislike.' So seventy men were killed from them on the day of Uhud, the number of the captives of the people of Badr."

I say: It was narrated in *al-Majma'* from Ali (peace be upon him), and al-Qummi recorded it in his commentary.

In *al-Majma'*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَلا تَحْسَبَنَّ الَّذِينَ قُتِلُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ** — "Do not suppose those who were slain in the way of Allah..." (3:169), from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him): "It was revealed concerning the martyrs of Badr and Uhud together."

I say: There are many narrations to this effect in *al-Durr al-Manthur* and elsewhere. You have already learned that the meaning of the verses is general, encompassing everyone truly or legally killed in the way of Allah. It is sometimes said that the verses were revealed concerning the martyrs of Bi'r Ma'unah, who were seventy or forty men from the companions of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) whom he sent to invite Amir ibn al-Tufayl and his people. They were at that water when they sent Abu Milhan al-Ansari to them with the message; they killed him first, then they fell upon the companions of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and fought them until they killed them all (may Allah be pleased with them).

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, from Imam al-Sadiq: "They are, by Allah, our Shia (شيعتنا) when their souls reached Paradise and they received honor from Allah, the Mighty and Majestic. They knew and were certain that they were upon the truth and upon the religion of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic. So they rejoiced in those who had not yet joined them from their brothers among the believers left behind."

I say: This is of the nature of "conceptual flow." Their knowing and being certain that they were upon the truth means they attained this through the "eye of certainty" (عين اليقين) after having attained it in the world through the "knowledge of certainty" (علم اليقين), not that they were in doubt or suspicion while in the world.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ahmad, Hannad, Abd ibn Humayd, Abu Dawud, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, al-Hakim—who graded it as *sahih*—and al-Bayhaqi in *al-Dala'il* narrated from Ibn Abbas: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'When your brothers were smitten at Uhud, Allah placed their souls in the crops of green birds that frequent the rivers of Paradise and eat of its fruits, then return to golden lamps suspended in the shade of the Throne.

When they found the goodness of their food, drink, and resting place, they said: "If only our brothers knew what Allah has done for us."' In another wording: 'They said: "We are alive in Paradise, being provided for, so that they may not lose interest in *jihad* nor recoil from war." Allah said: "I shall convey it to them on your behalf." So Allah revealed these verses: **وَلا تَحْسَبَنَّ الَّذِينَ قُتِلُوا** and what follows.'"

I say: There are many narrations with this meaning from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri, Abd Allah ibn Mas'ud, Abu al-Aliyah, Ibn Abbas, and others. In some: "in the forms of green birds" like the narration of Abu al-Aliyah; in some: "in green birds" like the narration of Abu Sa'id; and in some: "as green birds" like the narration of Ibn Mas'ud. The wordings are similar.

It has been narrated through the chains of the Imams of the People of the House (أهل البيت) that when this narration was presented to them, they denied it being from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). In some reports, they interpreted it. There is no doubt—considering the established and accepted principles—of the necessity of interpreting the narration if it is not rejected.

Furthermore, the narrations are not in the position of describing their state in the Paradise of the Hereafter; rather, what is intended is the Paradise of the intermediate realm (البرزخ) — *the state between death and resurrection*. The evidence for this is in the narration of Ibn Jarir from Mujahid: "They are provided from the fruits of Paradise and find its scent, but they are not in it." And in the narration of Ibn Jarir from al-Suddi: "The souls of the martyrs are in the crops of green birds in golden lamps suspended from the Throne; they graze morning and evening in Paradise and spend the night in the lamps."

You have already learned in the previous discussion on the intermediate realm (البرزخ) that the content of these two narrations is only consistent with the Paradise of this world, which is the *Barzakh*, not the Paradise of the Hereafter.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **الَّذِينَ اسْتَجابُوا لِلَّهِ** (3:172), Ibn Ishaq, Ibn Jarir, and al-Bayhaqi in *al-Dala'il* narrated from Abd Allah ibn Abi Bakr ibn Muhammad ibn Amr ibn Hazm: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) went out to Hamra' al-Asad. Abu Sufyan had resolved to return to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and his companions, saying: 'We returned before we could eradicate them; we shall certainly attack their remnants.' He was informed that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) had come out with his companions seeking them. This deterred Abu Sufyan and his companions. A caravan from Abd al-Qays passed by, and Abu Sufyan said to them: 'Inform Muhammad that we have resolved to return to his companions to eradicate them.'

When the caravan passed by the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) at Hamra' al-Asad, they informed him of what Abu Sufyan had said. The Messenger of Allah and the believers with him said: 'Allah is sufficient for us, and He is an excellent trustee.' Then Allah revealed regarding that: **الَّذِينَ اسْتَجابُوا لِلَّهِ وَالرَّسُولِ**."

I say: al-Qummi narrated this in detail in his commentary, mentioning that he (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) took with him to Hamra' al-Asad those of his companions who were wounded. In some narrations, he only took those who had been at Uhud, and the outcome is the same.

In the same source, Musa ibn Uqbah narrated in his *Maghazi* and al-Bayhaqi in *al-Dala'il* from Ibn Shihab: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) called the Muslims to the appointment with Abu Sufyan at Badr. Satan carried his friends among the people, and they went among the people frightening them, saying: 'We have been informed that they have gathered against you people like the night, hoping to fall upon you and plunder you; so beware, beware!' Allah protected the Muslims from Satan's intimidation, so they responded to Allah and His Messenger. They went out with their merchandise, saying: 'If we meet Abu Sufyan, he is the one we went out for; and if we do not meet him, we will sell our merchandise.' Badr was a marketplace frequented every year. They set out until they reached the season of Badr and fulfilled their needs. Abu Sufyan broke the appointment and did not come out, nor did his companions. Ibn Hammam passed by them and asked: 'Who are these?' They said: 'The Messenger of Allah and his companions waiting for Abu Sufyan and those of the Quraysh with him.' He went to the Quraysh and informed them, and Abu Sufyan was terrified and returned to Mecca. The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) returned to Medina with blessing and grace from Allah. That expedition was considered the 'Expedition of the Army of Sawiq' (جيش السويق), and it was in Sha'ban of the third year."

I say: It has been narrated through other than this chain, and al-Majma' narrated it in detail from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him). In it, it is stated that the verses were revealed regarding the "Lesser Badr." The "Army of Sawiq" refers to the army of Abu Sufyan, for he came out of Mecca with an army of Quraysh carrying loads of *sawiq* (parched grain). They camped outside Mecca, ate the *sawiq*, and then returned to Mecca when terror seized them from meeting the Muslims at Badr. The people called them the "Army of Sawiq" in mockery and derision.

Also in it, al-Nasa'i, Ibn Abi Hatim, and al-Tabarani narrated with a sound chain from Ikrimah from Ibn Abbas: "When the polytheists returned from Uhud, they said: 'You neither killed Muhammad nor took the young women; what a wretched thing you have done! Return!' The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) heard of this and summoned the Muslims. They responded until they reached Hamra' al-Asad or Bi'r Abi Utbah—Sufyan was in doubt. The polytheists said: 'We shall return next year.' The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) returned, and it was counted as an expedition. Then Allah revealed: **الَّذِينَ اسْتَجابُوا لِلَّهِ وَالرَّسُولِ**. Abu Sufyan had said to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): 'Your appointment is the season of Badr where you killed our companions.' As for the coward, he returned; as for the brave, he took the equipment for fighting and trade. They went there but found no one, so they traded. Then Allah revealed: **فَانْقَلَبُوا بِنِعْمَةٍ مِنَ اللهِ وَفَضْلٍ**."

I say: We have only cited this narration despite its contradictions for the sake of brevity and summary, which is effective in traditionary discourses by providing a comprehensive sample from every category. This is so the contemplative researcher may realize that what they mention as "occasions of revelation" (أسباب النزول) are all or mostly theoretical. This means they often narrate historical events and then join them with whatever verses of the Noble Quran can be applied to them, counting them as occasions of revelation. This sometimes leads to the fragmentation of a single verse or verses with a single context, then attributing each part to a separate, independent revelation, even if this causes the disruption of the verses' order and the invalidation of their context. This is one of the reasons for the weakness in the genre of narrations concerning the occasions of revelation.

Add to this what we mentioned at the beginning of this discourse—that the differences between schools of thought have an impact on the tone of these narrations and their direction toward justifying specific doctrines.

Furthermore, the political climates and the ruling environments in every age have a powerful effect on truths in terms of concealing or obscuring them. Therefore, the contemplative researcher must not neglect these factors that play a role in understanding the truths. And Allah is the Guide.

## (Historical Discourse)

The martyrs of the Muslims on the day of Uhud were seventy men. Here is the list of their names:

1. Hamzah ibn Abd al-Muttalib ibn Hashim.
2. Abd Allah ibn Jahsh.
3. Mus'ab ibn Umayr.
4. Shammas ibn Uthman. These four were the martyrs from the Emigrants (المهاجرين).
5. Amr ibn Mu'adh ibn al-Nu'man.
6. al-Harith ibn Anas ibn Rafi'.
7. Umarah ibn Ziyad ibn al-Sakan.
8. Salamah ibn Thabit ibn Waqsh.
9. Amr ibn Thabit ibn Waqsh.
10. Thabit ibn Waqsh.
11. Rifa'ah ibn Waqsh.
12. Husayl ibn Jabir Abu Hudhayfah al-Yaman.
13. Sayfi ibn Qayzi.
14. Hubab ibn Qayzi.
15. Abbad ibn Sahl.
16. al-Harith ibn Aws ibn Mu'adh.
17. Iyas ibn Aws.
18. Ubayd ibn al-Tayyihan.
19. Habib ibn Yazid ibn Taym.
20. Yazid ibn Hatib ibn Umayyah ibn Rafi'.
21. Abu Sufyan ibn al-Harith ibn Qays ibn Zayd.
22. Hanzalah ibn Abi Amir, who is "the one washed by the angels" (غسيل الملائكة).
23. Anis ibn Qatadah.
24. Abu Habbah ibn Amr ibn Thabit.
25. Abd Allah ibn Jubayr ibn al-Nu'man, the commander of the archers.
26. Abu Sa'd Khaythamah ibn Khaythamah.
27. Abd Allah ibn Salamah.
28. Subay' ibn Hatib ibn al-Harith.
29. Amr ibn Qays.
30. Qays ibn Amr ibn Qays.
31. Thabit ibn Amr ibn Yazid.
32. Amir ibn Mukhallad.
33. Abu Hubayrah ibn al-Harith ibn Alqamah ibn Amr.
34. Amr ibn Mutarrif ibn Alqamah ibn Amr.
35. Aws ibn Thabit ibn al-Mundhir, brother of Hassan ibn Thabit.
36. Anas ibn al-Nadr, uncle of Anas ibn Malik, the servant of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).
37. Qays ibn Mukhallad.
38. Kaysan, a slave of the Banu al-Najjar.
39. Sulaym ibn al-Harith.
40. Nu'man ibn Abd Amr.
41. Kharijah ibn Zayd ibn Abi Zuhayr.
42. Sa'd ibn al-Rabi' ibn Amr ibn Abi Zuhayr.
43. Aws ibn al-Arqam.
44. Malik ibn Sinan of the Banu Khudrah, the father of Abu Sa'id al-Khudri.
45. Sa'id ibn Suwayd.
46. Utbah ibn Rabi'.
47. Tha'labah ibn Sa'd ibn Malik.
48. Saqf ibn Farwah ibn al-Badi.
49. Abd Allah ibn Amr ibn Wahb.
50. Damrah, an ally of the Banu Tarif.
51. Nawfal ibn Abd Allah.
52. Abbas ibn Ubadah.
53. Nu'man ibn Malik ibn Tha'labah.
54. al-Mujadhdhar ibn Ziyad.
55. Ubadah ibn al-Hashas. Nu'man, al-Mujadhdhar, and Ubadah were buried in a single grave.
56. Rifa'ah ibn Amr.
57. Abd Allah ibn Amr of the Banu Haram.
58. Amr ibn al-Jamuh of the Banu Haram. They were buried in a single grave.
59. Khallad ibn Amr ibn al-Jamuh.
60. Abu Ayman, the client (مولى) of Amr ibn al-Jamuh.
61. Sulaym ibn Amr ibn Hadidah.
62. Antarah, the client of Sulaym.
63. Sahl ibn Qays ibn Abi Ka'b.
64. Dhakwan ibn Abd Qays.
65. Ubayd ibn al-Mu'alla.
66. Malik ibn Tumaylah.
67. Harith ibn Adi ibn Kharashah.
68. Malik ibn Iyas.
69. Iyas ibn Adi.
70. Amr ibn Iyas.

These are seventy men according to what Ibn Hisham mentioned in the *Sirah* of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).
